county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Adjuntas, established 1815, carved from Utuado. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Adjuntas, known as 'La Ciudad del Gigante Dormido' (Sleeping Giant City), was carved from Utuado in 1815 in the central mountain (Cordillera Central) coffee-growing region. Catholic parish Parroquia San Joaquín y Santa Ana holds registers from the early 19th century — check FamilySearch microfilm collection for digitized baptism and marriage records. Coffee hacienda records and notarial protocols at AGPR document land transactions among highland settlers. The municipio's remote mountain location means some families remained in the same barrios for generations, making barrio-level research essential.

source_profiles.source_name: Adjuntas Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Adjuntas municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1815.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Adjuntas Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Adjuntas / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Adjuntas including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Adjuntas Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Adjuntas district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1815. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Adjuntas Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Adjuntas. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Adjuntas Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Adjuntas district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Adjuntas district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Adjuntas Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Adjuntas / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Adjuntas. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Aguada, established 1692, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Aguada is one of Puerto Rico's oldest settlements, traditionally associated with Columbus's 1493 landing (disputed with Aguadilla). Founded as a parish by 1692, early church records at Parroquia San Francisco de Asís date to the late 1600s and are among the island's oldest surviving registers. The Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR) holds notarial protocols from the colonial period. As a parent municipio to Aguadilla (1775), Rincón (1771), and indirectly to Moca and others, pre-1775 records for the entire northwestern region begin here. Check Spanish military records — Aguada was a key defensive point against foreign incursions.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Aguadilla, established 1775, carved from Aguada. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Aguadilla separated from Aguada in 1775 and became an important port city on the northwest coast. Ramey Air Force Base (1939-1973) brought significant population shifts — military personnel records at NARA complement local sources. Catholic parish Parroquia San Carlos Borromeo has records from the late 18th century. The municipio served as parent to Isabela (1819) and Moca (1772), so pre-separation records are found in Aguadilla and ultimately Aguada. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of church registers. Corsican and Mallorcan immigration in the 19th century is well-documented in passenger lists and notarial protocols.

source_profiles.source_name: Aguadilla Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Colección Puertorriqueña, UPR / Biblioteca Nacional / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers published in or covering Aguadilla. Puerto Rico's newspaper tradition began in the early 19th century. Papers include birth/death/marriage announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news. La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette) published government notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some newspaper runs are incomplete. The Colección Puertorriqueña at UPR Río Piedras has the most extensive holdings. Chronicling America has digitized selected Puerto Rico newspapers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Aguas Buenas, established 1838, carved from Caguas. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Aguas Buenas, known as 'La Ciudad de las Aguas Claras,' was carved from Caguas in 1838. Located in the eastern interior mountains, its Catholic parish Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Monserrate holds registers from the mid-19th century. Pre-1838 records are found in Caguas parish records. The municipio's economy centered on tobacco and minor crops — hacienda records and merchant account books at AGPR provide genealogical data. Barrios like Bayamoncito, Caguitas, and Sonadora have distinct settlement histories. Check notarial protocols for land partitions (particiones) that document family relationships across generations.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Aibonito, established 1824, carved from Coamo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Aibonito, the highest municipio in Puerto Rico at over 2,400 feet elevation, was separated from Coamo in 1824. Known as 'La Ciudad de las Flores,' its cool climate attracted Spanish settlers and later became a retreat. Parroquia San José records begin in the 1820s. The Battle of Asomante (1898) during the Spanish-American War occurred here — US military records at NARA document this transitional period. Pre-1824 records are in Coamo, one of PR's oldest parishes. Coffee cultivation and cattle ranching dominate hacienda records. Check civil registry (Registro Demográfico) from 1885 for vital records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Arecibo, established 1614, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Arecibo, founded circa 1614, is one of Puerto Rico's original settlements and a major north-coast city. Parroquia San Felipe Apóstol holds some of the island's oldest church registers, with baptisms from the 1600s. As a parent to Barceloneta, Hatillo, Camuy, and indirectly many other municipios, Arecibo is a critical research hub for the entire north-central region. The Archivo Histórico de Arecibo supplements AGPR holdings. Sugar and rum industry records are extensive. Arecibo's large enslaved population makes Freedmen-era records and slave registers particularly important. The Arecibo Observatory area was historically part of Barrio Esperanza.

source_profiles.source_name: Arecibo Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Arecibo including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Arroyo, established 1855, carved from Guayama. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Arroyo was carved from Guayama in 1855 and became a prosperous sugar port on the southeast coast. The former customs house reflects its importance as a shipping center. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen records begin in the 1850s — pre-1855 records are in Guayama. Sugar plantation records at AGPR document both landowners and enslaved laborers. Samuel Morse's involvement with a telegraph cable between Arroyo and Vieques in 1858 is documented in historical records. The municipio had a significant Afro-Puerto Rican population tied to the sugar economy. Check shipping manifests at AGPR for trade-related genealogical data.

source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Arroyo municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1855.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Arroyo / AGPR, San Juan

source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Arroyo Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Arroyo district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1855. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Arroyo Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Arroyo. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Arroyo Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Arroyo district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Arroyo district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Arroyo Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Arroyo / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Arroyo. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Arroyo Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Arroyo including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Añasco, established 1728, carved from San Germán. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Añasco was established as a parish in 1728, carved from San Germán, making it one of the older west-coast settlements. Parroquia San Antonio Abad de Padua has church registers from the early 18th century. The Río Grande de Añasco valley supported sugar plantations — enslaved persons records, coartación documents, and liberto registers at AGPR are essential for tracing African-descended families. French and Corsican immigrants settled here in the early 19th century. Notarial protocols document complex sugar hacienda transactions. As the Grito de Lares (1868) involved residents from this area, check military tribunal records for participants.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Barceloneta, established 1881, carved from Arecibo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Barceloneta, carved from Arecibo in 1881, is a coastal municipio on the north shore known for its pineapple industry. Named for Barcelona, Spain, reflecting its settlers' origins. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen holds records from the 1880s — pre-1881 records are in Arecibo's extensive parish archives. As one of the later-formed municipios, civil registry records from 1885 overlap closely with its formation. The pharmaceutical industry established in the 20th century brought population changes. Check Arecibo notarial protocols for pre-separation land and family records. Barrio Galateo Bajo and Florida barrio have distinct settlement patterns.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Barranquitas, established 1803, carved from Aibonito, Coamo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Barranquitas, carved from Aibonito and Coamo in 1803, sits in the central mountains and is the birthplace of Luis Muñoz Rivera (1859) and birthplace site of Luis Muñoz Marín, Puerto Rico's first elected governor. Parroquia San Antonio de Padua holds records from the early 19th century. The Muñoz Rivera Museum provides historical context. Coffee hacienda records at AGPR document highland agricultural families. As parent to Orocovis (1825), early records cover a wider area. The 1898 Spanish-American War transition records at NARA include military government documents affecting this region. Check notarial protocols for coffee farm transactions and inheritance divisions.
